quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Law of Segregation"?

options.text: v = Vmax[S]/(Km + [S]); describes the relationship between substrate concentration and reaction rate for many enzymes
Using the exergonic hydrolysis of ATP to drive an endergonic reaction, often through phosphorylation of a substrate
A species whose impact on community structure is disproportionately large relative to its abundance (e.g., sea otters, wolves in Yellowstone)
Each diploid organism has two alleles for each gene; these separate during meiosis so each gamete carries only one allele

quiz_questions.explanation: "Law of Segregation" is defined as: Each diploid organism has two alleles for each gene; these separate during meiosis so each gamete carries only one allele. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Law of Independent Assortment"?

options.text: A molecule resembling the substrate that competes for the active site; increases apparent Km but does not affect Vmax (can be overcome by more substrate)
The variety of life at three levels: genetic diversity (within species), species diversity (number and relative abundance), and ecosystem diversity
S = cAᶻ; the number of species increases with habitat area, where z ≈ 0.3 for islands and c is a constant
Genes on different (non-homologous) chromosomes sort independently into gametes during meiosis

quiz_questions.explanation: "Law of Independent Assortment" is defined as: Genes on different (non-homologous) chromosomes sort independently into gametes during meiosis. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Dominant and recessive"?

options.text: A dominant allele is expressed in heterozygotes; a recessive allele is expressed only in homozygotes (aa)
A 3-carbon sugar produced by the Calvin cycle; the direct product used to build glucose, amino acids, and fatty acids
Failure of chromosomes to separate properly during meiosis, resulting in gametes with abnormal chromosome numbers (aneuploidy)
A shared derived character that defines a clade; evidence of common ancestry (e.g., feathers in birds)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Dominant and recessive" is defined as: A dominant allele is expressed in heterozygotes; a recessive allele is expressed only in homozygotes (aa). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Punnett square"?

options.text: No mutation, no migration, random mating, no selection, large population size; violations drive evolution
Non-native organisms that spread aggressively in new environments, outcompeting native species (e.g., kudzu, cane toads, zebra mussels)
A grid used to predict genotype and phenotype ratios in offspring from a particular cross
A signaling molecule (hormone, neurotransmitter, growth factor) that binds specifically to a receptor protein

quiz_questions.explanation: "Punnett square" is defined as: A grid used to predict genotype and phenotype ratios in offspring from a particular cross. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Test cross"?

options.text: Crossing an individual of unknown genotype (dominant phenotype) with a homozygous recessive (aa) to determine whether the unknown is AA or Aa
Regulatory molecules bind to a site other than the active site, changing the enzyme's conformation and activity; can be activating or inhibiting
A molecule resembling the substrate that competes for the active site; increases apparent Km but does not affect Vmax (can be overcome by more substrate)
Multiple genes contribute to a single trait, producing continuous variation (e.g., skin color, height, intelligence)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Test cross" is defined as: Crossing an individual of unknown genotype (dominant phenotype) with a homozygous recessive (aa) to determine whether the unknown is AA or Aa. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Each diploid organism has two alleles for each gene; these separate during meiosis so each gamete carries only one allele"?

options.text: Allele frequency
Chitin
FADH₂
Law of Segregation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Law of Segregation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Genes on different (non-homologous) chromosomes sort independently into gametes during meiosis"?

options.text: Law of Independent Assortment
Linked genes
Starch
Photorespiration

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Law of Independent Assortment".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A dominant allele is expressed in heterozygotes; a recessive allele is expressed only in homozygotes (aa)"?

options.text: Tertiary structure
Energy coupling
Dominant and recessive
Exponential growth

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Dominant and recessive".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A grid used to predict genotype and phenotype ratios in offspring from a particular cross"?

options.text: Ligand
Final electron acceptor
Gel electrophoresis
Punnett square

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Punnett square".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Crossing an individual of unknown genotype (dominant phenotype) with a homozygous recessive (aa) to determine whether the unknown is AA or Aa"?

options.text: Linked genes
Test cross
Outgroup
Frameshift mutation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Test cross".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Mendelian Genetics is accurate?

options.text: Mendelian Genetics describes inheritance patterns based on Gregor Mendel's laws of segregation (each organism carries two alleles that separate during gamete formation) and independent assortment (genes on different chromosomes are inherited...
Photosystems II and I absorb photons, exciting electrons that pass through electron transport chains.
NADH and FADH₂ donate electrons to protein complexes (I-IV), which pump H⁺ from the matrix to the intermembrane space, creating a proton gradient.
Three turns of the Calvin cycle fix 3 CO₂ to produce one net G3P molecule, consuming 9 ATP and 6 NADPH.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Mendelian Genetics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Mendelian Genetics?

options.text: The enzyme RuBisCO catalyzes carbon fixation (CO₂ + RuBP → 2 molecules of 3-PGA), making it the most abundant protein on Earth.
Dominant alleles mask recessive alleles in heterozygotes.
The central dogma of molecular biology—DNA → RNA → Protein—describes the flow of genetic information in cells.

The three stages—reception (ligand binding), transduction (signal relay/amplification through phosphorylation cascades), and response (gene expression, enzyme activation, or cell movement)—allow cells to respond to their environment.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Mendelian Genetics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Mendelian Genetics?

options.text: Saturated vs. unsaturated fatty acids differ in the presence of C=C double bonds, affecting fluidity and melting point.
Monohybrid crosses (Aa × Aa → 3:1 phenotypic ratio) and dihybrid crosses (AaBb × AaBb → 9:3:3:1 ratio) predict offspring ratios, testable with chi-square analysis.
Populations are characterized by density, age structure, survivorship curves (Type I, II, III), and life history strategies (r-selected vs.

The Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (p² + 2pq + q² = 1, p + q = 1) serves as a null hypothesis: populations are in equilibrium only when there is no mutation, migration, selection, drift, or non-random mating.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Mendelian Genetics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Mendelian Genetics?

options.text: Genomic technologies (DNA sequencing, gene editing with CRISPR-Cas9, and bioinformatics) have revolutionized biology, medicine, agriculture, and forensics.
Competitive inhibitors resemble the substrate and compete for the active site, while noncompetitive inhibitors bind elsewhere and reduce catalytic activity.
NADH and FADH₂ donate electrons to protein complexes (I-IV), which pump H⁺ from the matrix to the intermembrane space, creating a proton gradient.

Mendelian Genetics describes inheritance patterns based on Gregor Mendel's laws of segregation (each organism carries two alleles that separate during gamete formation) and independent assortment (genes on different chromosomes are inherited...

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Mendelian Genetics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Mendelian Genetics?

options.text: Recombination frequency between linked genes (calculated from testcross offspring) indicates the distance between genes on a chromosome, measured in centimorgans (cM) or map units.
NADH and FADH₂ donate electrons to protein complexes (I-IV), which pump H⁺ from the matrix to the intermembrane space, creating a proton gradient.
Dominant alleles mask recessive alleles in heterozygotes.
Parsimony (fewest evolutionary changes) and maximum likelihood methods are used to determine the most probable tree topology.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Mendelian Genetics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Mendelian Genetics?

options.text: Rising atmospheric CO₂ levels increasing carbon fixation rates in some plants (CO₂ fertilization effect) but potentially decreasing nutritional quality of crops
Genetic counseling using Punnett squares and pedigree analysis to calculate the probability of offspring inheriting genetic diseases like cystic fibrosis
α-amanitin (death cap mushroom toxin) inhibiting RNA polymerase II, blocking transcription and causing fatal liver failure within days
Metabolic syndrome and diabetes: dysregulation of glucose metabolism affecting cellular respiration, insulin signaling, and energy storage pathways

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Mendelian Genetics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Mendelian Genetics?

options.text: Biofuel production engineering algae with enhanced Calvin cycle efficiency to convert CO₂ into lipids for biodiesel
Agricultural selective breeding applying Mendelian genetics to develop crop varieties with desired traits (disease resistance, yield) over multiple generations

Epinephrine (adrenaline) signaling cascade: one epinephrine molecule activates many G-proteins, each activating adenylyl cyclase producing millions of cAMP molecules, amplifying the fight-or-flight response
C4 and CAM photosynthesis as evolutionary adaptations to minimize photorespiration in hot, dry environments (corn, sugarcane, cacti, pineapple)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Mendelian Genetics.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Mendelian Genetics applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Forensic genetics using Mendelian inheritance patterns to establish paternity or identify family relationships from DNA evidence
Whale evolution documented through a remarkable fossil series: Pakicetus (land mammal) → Ambulocetus (walking whale) → Dorudon (aquatic) → modern whales over ~50 million years
Metabolic interconnections: Krebs cycle intermediates serve as precursors for amino acid synthesis, fatty acid synthesis, and gluconeogenesis
C4 and CAM photosynthesis as evolutionary adaptations to minimize photorespiration in hot, dry environments (corn, sugarcane, cacti, pineapple)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Mendelian Genetics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Mendelian Genetics?

options.text: Citrate as a food additive and preservative, derived from the first intermediate of the Krebs cycle, produced industrially by Aspergillus niger fermentation
Genetic counseling using Punnett squares and pedigree analysis to calculate the probability of offspring inheriting genetic diseases like cystic fibrosis
HPV vaccines (Gardasil) preventing cervical cancer by protecting against viral proteins (E6, E7) that inactivate tumor suppressors p53 and Rb
Genetic rescue of the Florida panther: introducing 8 female Texas pumas in 1995 restored genetic diversity and tripled the population from ~25 to ~200 individuals

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Mendelian Genetics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Mendelian Genetics in practice?

options.text: Rising atmospheric CO₂ levels increasing carbon fixation rates in some plants (CO₂ fertilization effect) but potentially decreasing nutritional quality of crops
Targeted cancer therapy: Herceptin (trastuzumab) blocking the HER2 receptor in breast cancers that overexpress this oncogene protein
Agricultural selective breeding applying Mendelian genetics to develop crop varieties with desired traits (disease resistance, yield) over multiple generations
Endosymbiotic theory evidence: mitochondria and chloroplasts have double membranes, own circular DNA, 70S ribosomes, and replicate independently

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Mendelian Genetics.
